title: Proposal
text: TMAO is a metabolite know to increase risk of cardiovascular disease and certain gut bacteria are known to be TMA-producers, affecting levels of TMAO in individuals based on diet. In this project, we analyze publicly available 16s rRNA data to determine the relative abundances of TMA-producing bacteria in those consuming vegetarian diets and those consuming omnivorous diets to determine differences and risk potential.

title: Proposal
text: TMAO is a metabolite know to increase risk of cardiovascular disease and certain gut bacteria are known to be TMA-producers, affecting levels of TMAO in individuals based on diet. title: Proposal
text:
2 Dataset(s)

Source: NCBI
Sample size: 57
Key variables: Diets (Vegetarian and Omnivore)
License/usage notes: Downloaded from publicly available dataset on NCBI

title: Proposal
text:
3 Planned analysis
Describe your plan in bullets: - Organize all 16S V3–V4 sequences (FASTQ/FASTA) from SRA - DADA2 learns sequencing error rates from dataset - Remove noise and correct sequencing errors - Collapse sequences into ASVs - Assign ASVs to known TMA-producing taxa using SILVA database - Convert raw counts to relative abundance - Generate table of sample summary and top 5 TMA-related taxa for each sample

title: Proposal
text:
4 Deliverables

Stacked bar plot: Microbial composition by diet group
Boxplot: Relative abundance of TMA-associated bacteria by diet
Shannon Plot: Within-sample diversity across diet groups
PCoA Plot: Community-level differences between diet groups
Sample Summary: Sample metadata and group assignments
TMA-Associated Bacteria: Abundance of TMA-related taxa across samples

3 Collaboration rules (simple)

Pull before you start work.

Only one person edits the same .qmd file at a time.
Use Issues (optional) for tasks/bugs.

title: The Effect of Omnivorous v. Vegetarian Diet on Gut Microbiota Diversity and the Abundance of TMA-Producing Bacteria in the Human Gut Microbiome
text:
Research Question: Do vegetarians and omnivores differ in gut microbiota composition and in the relative abundance of bacterial taxa associated with microbial TMA production?
Hypothesis: Omnivores will have a higher relative abundance of gut microbial taxa associated with microbial TMA production compared to vegetarians.
Our data is a collection of 16s rDNA amplicon of human vegetarian. Dataset is obtained from NCBI, and contains multiple 300-301 long DNA sequences for 57 healthy volunteers based on fecal sample. The raw sequences are a collection of 16s rDNA amplicon of human vegetarian/ominovores. We conducted quality control and generation of ASV once we acquired our data, and then assigned taxonomies to each. This was followed by data normalization, diversity analysis, TMA-producing taxa analysis, and visualization of our data. We intend to deliver a collection of figures including a stacked bar plot (to llustrate the bacterial composition of the different diet groups), a boxplot (to compare the relative abundance of TMA-associated bacteria by diet group), an Alpha diversity plot (showcasing diversity analysis (Shannon index) between diet groups), and a PCoA Plot (showcasing visualization of differences between the diet groups based off of their gut bacteria), as well as a collection of tables including one to summarize the samples, one to illustrate levels of TMA-related bacteria, and one to showcasing statistical test results.
